write.post: Code review culture beats tooling because a linter can't tell you the abstraction is wrong. The best static analysis flags null derefs; it never asks why this service owns that data. Buy the tools, but if reviewers rubber-stamp under deadline pressure, you're paying for the illusion of review.

output.post: Code review culture beats tooling because a linter can't tell you the abstraction is wrong. The best static analysis flags null derefs; it never asks why this service owns that data. Buy the tools, but if reviewers rubber-stamp under deadline pressure, you're paying for the illusion of review.

Code review culture beats tooling every time. A linter catches unused imports; it doesn't catch the reviewer who rubber-stamps a 400-line PR in 90 seconds. The best tooling I've seen made bad reviews faster, not fewer. Small PRs, real questions, and reviewers who actually run the branch move defect rates more than any tool you can install.

Code review culture beats tooling because tools flag what changed, not whether it should have. A linter catches an unused import in 50ms; it never asks why the function exists. Teams that treat review as shared ownership ship fewer regressions than teams that bolt on more checks and skim the diff.

Code review culture matters more than tooling because a linter can flag an unused import in 50ms but it can't ask why a function exists. I've watched teams add three static analysis tools and still ship the same class of bug, while a team with one reviewer who asks "what happens when this input is null" cut escaped defects by 40%. Tools catch the 20% of issues that are mechanical; people catch the 80% that are about intent. Buy the tools, but the review habit is what moves the number.

Code review culture beats tooling because a linter can't tell you the abstraction is wrong. The best static analysis flags null derefs; it never asks why this service owns that data. Buy the tools, but if reviewers rubber-stamp under deadline pressure, you're paying for the illusion of review.
